There was a chosen group of Jewish believers who had righteousness and life through faith in Jesus Christ. These were kept according to God’s election of grace. If this election came from grace, then it could not come from works, whether those works were done or foreseen. Every truly good disposition in a fallen person must be the result of God's grace bestowed on him; therefore, it cannot be the cause. Salvation from beginning to end must either be by grace or by debt. These things are so directly opposed to each other that they cannot be combined. God glorifies his grace by changing the hearts and temperaments of rebellious people. How then should they wonder and praise him! The Jewish nation was like a people in a deep sleep, without knowledge of their danger or concern about it; having no sense of their need for the Savior, or of being on the verge of eternal ruin. David, by the Spirit, foretold the sufferings of Christ from his own people, the Jews, and also foretold the dreadful judgments of God upon them because of it. This teaches us how to understand other prayers of David against his enemies; they are prophecies of the judgments of God, not expressions of his own anger. Divine curses will last a long time; and we have our eyes darkened if we are consumed by worldly concerns.

The gospel is the greatest blessing in every place where it is found. As the righteous rejection of the unbelieving Jews created an opportunity for a large number of Gentiles to be reconciled to God and at peace with him, the future receiving of the Jews into the church would be like a general resurrection of the dead from sin to a life of righteousness. Abraham was the root of the church. The Jews continued as branches of this tree until, as a nation, they rejected the Messiah; after that, their relationship to Abraham and to God was, as it were, cut off. The Gentiles were grafted into this tree in their place; being admitted into the church of God. Many people became heirs of Abraham’s faith, holiness, and blessedness. It is natural for everyone to be wild by nature. Conversion is like grafting wild branches onto a good olive tree. Wild olives were often grafted into the fruitful one when it began to decay, and this not only produced fruit but caused the decaying olive to revive and flourish. The Gentiles, by free grace, had been grafted in to share advantages. They should therefore beware of self-confidence, and every kind of pride or ambition; lest, having only a dead faith and an empty profession, they turn from God and forfeit their privileges. If we stand at all, it is by faith; we are guilty and helpless in ourselves, and must be humble, watchful, afraid of deceiving ourselves, or being overcome by temptation. Not only are we initially justified by faith, but kept to the end in that justified state by faith alone; yet, by a faith which is not isolated, but which works through love for God and people.

Of all judgments, spiritual judgments are the most severe; the apostle is speaking about these here. The restoration of the Jews is, in the course of events, far less improbable than the call of the Gentiles to be the children of Abraham; and though others now possess these privileges, it will not hinder their being admitted again. By rejecting the gospel, and by their anger at its being preached to the Gentiles, the Jews became enemies to God; yet they are still to be favored for the sake of their pious fathers. Though at present they are enemies to the gospel because of their hatred for the Gentiles, that will not always exist, and God’s love for their fathers will be remembered. True grace does not seek to limit God's favor. Those who find mercy themselves should try to ensure that others also obtain mercy. Not that the Jews will be restored to have their priesthood, temple, and ceremonies again; an end has been put to all these; but they are to believe in Christ, become one flock with the Gentiles, under Christ the Great Shepherd. The captivities of Israel, their dispersion, and being shut out from the church, are symbols of a believer’s corrections for wrongdoing; and the continued care of the Lord towards that people, and the final mercy and blessed restoration intended for them, show God's patience and love.

The apostle Paul knew the mysteries of the kingdom of God as well as anyone ever has; yet he confesses that he does not understand. Despairing to find the bottom, he humbly sits down at the edge and worships the depth. Those who know most in this imperfect state feel their own weakness most. There is not only depth in God’s plans, but riches—abundance of things that are precious and valuable. God's plans are complete; they have not only depth and height, but breadth and length, and knowledge beyond measure. There is a vast distance and disproportion between God and man, between the Creator and the creature, which forever prevents us from knowing his ways. What person can teach God how to govern the world? The apostle worships the sovereignty of God’s plans. All things in heaven and earth, especially those that relate to our salvation, that belong to our peace, are all from him by way of creation, through him by way of providence, so they may be for him in their end. From God, as the Source and Origin of all; through Christ, to God, as the goal. These include all of God’s relationships with his creatures; if all things originate from Him and are through Him, then all should be for Him. Whatever begins, let God's glory be the end: especially let us worship him when we speak of God’s plans and actions. The saints in heaven never argue, but always praise.
